All right, this is the care and feeding of a client, basically. You know, there's a proverb that says that don't despise the ox just because he crafts in the barn. I'm paraphrasing because a lot of wealth comes from the strength of the ox. Well, clients are a pain in the butt. And they're also a lot of fun, and they're also the people that are going to pay you to do wonderful things. So working with them and nurturing that relationship is everything. Remember what we said about how you should be able to feel that you can spend at least as much time and money keeping a client as you did getting him in the first place. That is really sage advice. You need to think about that as you're working with your clients. So okay, you got a new client. What are you going to do? All right? Signing in was very costly for you in terms of time, effort, and so forth. I just covered point number two here. Point number three is far easier to increase. Not only should you be willing to invest the same time and effort in a client to keep him as you did to get him, you should invest the same amount of time in increasing the involvement that you have with each client and the revenue you derive from that client. And we've touched on this in a million different ways the last two days. But the whole idea is finding ways to make the pie bigger and to increase, broaden and deepen your relationship, your involvement with the client's business. I do that by trying to figure out ways to make myself indispensable. I have gotten to a point with one client where I feel, well, he told me this. And Martin is not one who's going to give compliments easily, and he's certainly not one who's going to give away negotiating points easily. But at one point I said something about being frustrated, and he said, well Clayton, if you leave, I might as well shut the company completely down. You know? Now that's after some 10 or 15 years of intentionally trying to make myself indispensable. So it was part of a plan. You know? And how did I do that? Well, I did that by deepening my involvement, being curious, asking about response rates, asking about renewal rates, calling up, with nobody asking me to do it, calling customer service and trying to buy a three-year subscription when I knew that he didn't offer one. So looking for opportunities and being a champion of the client. I'm not just here to write copy. I'm not just here to collect a royalty. I'm here to help your company grow. Get that in your mind. Get that in your head. That's your role. And once you get that, then you're going to see opportunities in each client's company to help him grow. Okay? These next few slides, I'm a little uncomfortable with the images. I don't know why, but somehow I think you should have been a guy and a girl, maybe. But anyway, position your first assignment as first dates. Okay? When Lane Lowery came to me, and I forget how he'd heard of me, but he came to me. This is the guy with health resources. And he said, come over to Birmingham. I'd like to talk to you about doing some work together. And I said, okay. So I came over and we talked. I could tell he really needed somebody, and I felt I could help him. But I said, you know, I don't work on individual assignments. I'm not going to take an assignment to write a package for oral chelation. I'm not interested in that. I said, I'm not a cheap date, basically. Okay? It's courtship, you know? And so what I said was basically, let's have a first date. Okay? I will write one promotion for you on a royalty basis. If I like you at the end of the project, and if you like me at the end of the project, then I'm going to want a long-term contract. And it's going to include, you know, the following things. It's going to include a monthly retainer. It's going to include a royalty on everything I do. Okay? And so I had his buy-in to that before I did anything else with him. He said, absolutely. That's what I need. I need somebody to. And so this became a relationship where once a quarter, Carleen would come up, Lane would come over to Atlanta, and we would get hotel rooms at a hotel there, and we would spend a whole weekend just talking about strategic issues in the company, how we could improve all of his numbers, you know? And those were some fun times. I mean, we had a ball, and because they were just nice people, so it was fun on top of everything else. But we were strategic consultants in that company, and we were helping him with staff issues, customer service issues, stuff that had nothing to do with direct response or direct mail, you know, which was our big thing at the time. We were doing catalogs, websites, all kinds of stuff. So it deepened our involvement with the company, and it was a wonderful way to go. So letting a client know, now, on a lot of projects, especially the newer writers here, you are going to want the fee-only job as a bridge to getting where you're going. You are going to want some of the fee-plus royalty things to get where you're going. But there will come a time, and it won't be that long, where you'll be meeting with somebody and you look at them and you go, he has no infrastructure. He doesn't know how to do this. I could write in the best copy in the world, and he wouldn't know how to print it. He wouldn't know how to get it out there. He wouldn't know how to pick the list. He wouldn't know how to get the media online. He wouldn't know how to get the ISPs to go ahead and deliver his stuff to people's inboxes instead of their spam folders. He wouldn't know how to do any of this stuff. That's a huge opportunity. That's an opportunity to do a wonderful deal and to say, okay, let's date. We're going to talk about chemistry a little bit, but chemistry is very important, so that's why I do the first date. I want the opportunity to work with a guy. If he's a royal pain, I don't want to work with him again. Life's too short. If he's incompetent or if his staff is incompetent and then defensive about being incompetent, then I'm not going to want to work with him. If they're open to criticism, to critiques, to suggestions, and actually get them implemented, then this might be the start of something big. Creating those expectations right at the very beginning sets up the sale. We did the first promotion, did land office business, did really well with it, and at that point he would have pretty much signed anything I put in front of him. One thing happened with security rare coin back in the day, back in the 80s. He called me up to hire me to write one package and I told him, I will do that. I wrote the package. I didn't tell him about the whole first date idea. I hadn't developed it yet, but I wrote that one package for him. It worked really well. I wrote another package for him really well. It was only on the basis of that relationship, and I liked working with a guy, that I was able to say, hey, let me take a deeper look at your company. I think I've told you that story probably more than once. I looked at how he was selling to his customers, thought I could do better, asked him if I could take a crack at it. He was doing $360,000 a month. We did $3.6 million on my very first effort to that file. Then at that point he called up and he said, you have to come to work for me full time. I said, you can't afford me. He said, yes I can. My number at the time was 100,000 a year. I was living in Prescott. I love Prescott, Arizona. He said, well, how much money do you want? I said, quarter of a million dollars. He goes, okay. What else? I said, 5% of sales. Okay. What else? I got to fire a bunch of clients so I want $40,000 cash. Okay. What else? I want a Cadillac. Okay. What else? I said, I want you to make the down payment on a new house for me. Okay. Every time he says, okay, I'm going, holy crap, I should have started it a million a year. That was all based on the concept of the first aid, even though that isn't the way we set it up. I've worked with them a few times. It looks good. This could be the start of something big. So yeah, and it can go farther than just the retainer situation, the partnership. It can end up in you owning the company, or at least a nice chunk of it. So what are you looking for? What are you looking for? If you're going to get into close relationship, if you're going to get into bed with somebody, your first chemistry, if you dread talking to this guy on the phone, if he's annoying, if he's pushy, if there's anything about him that doesn't work with you, that's not a good sign because you're going to go through some tough times together. You're going to go through some great times together, but also tough ones. You need to feel that you're simpatico enough that you can do that with this client. Anybody who's been doing this for any period of time knows, has had the experience of hooking up with a client and it just wasn't working. You just didn't communicate on the same level. You don't know what it was, but it wasn't working. So got to ask yourself, is there chemistry there? Is there a desire for the partnership? It's very difficult to get somebody who's running his own company his own way to say, well, yeah, I'll make a commitment to you as a copywriter. I won't use anyone else. You can be exclusive to me. He's got to have the desire. He's got to have the desire to grow his company. Now, Wendy's laughing. She knows what I'm going to say. We had this client. Carline had this guy too. And I think Laurie might have mentioned him. Anyway, so I wrote several promotions for this guy and he liked what we did and he came to town to visit and we went out to lunch and he said to me, I don't want to grow my company really. I don't want it bigger. I'm at a comfortable level now. I don't have to work too hard. I just, I don't care about getting bigger. I resigned the account right then. Because if he didn't want to get bigger, that means he wasn't going to mail maximum numbers of what I wrote for him, even if it was good, even if it was breaking even or better. And this was not someone I wanted to be in partnership with. So there's got to be a desire for growth. At Blanchard and Company, it was an interesting situation. Jim wanted to grow the company and sell it. And he told me that when he recruited me. His wife, Jackie, loved the fact that the company was more like a family. It was a small group of people who loved each other and loved working together. And that's what she wanted. Jim made the decisions though. So I didn't know that she didn't want the company to grow. So I come in and now I'm dealing with a client whose wife hates the fact that I'm there. But Jim was really great because he didn't care. He had a plan and it didn't matter whether Jackie liked it or not. So he always insulated me. But you've got to make sure that the desire is there. Do they have a list? We talked about this yesterday and also today, in the quality of the list and the various parameters of the list. How about infrastructure? Do they have a video production studio in the company? Are they capable of doing podcasts internally? If not, is their production facility close enough by to make it convenient? Do they have people working on the ISPs to make sure emails are delivered? Do they have somebody who can rent lists and identify good lists versus bad lists? How about IT? How about reporting? There's no more uncomfortable situation than to be working in a black hole, okay? Where you're writing copy like crazy, you're putting it out there, they're producing it, they're putting it in the mail and there are no numbers coming back at you. Especially at the time of the internet, right? I had a client where it took me four months to get a simple report that told me the relative, the percent of click-throughs on every subject line that I wrote. And it's important. I mean, you get this feedback, you get the status. Why they call this direct response marketing? It's a science. It's why we test. And if you're not getting data, how in the world are you gonna test anything, right? So if he doesn't have an IT department that's set up and able to give you the reports you need, that's a major red flag. If I ran into a company like that, I'd probably go to them and I'd say, look, I'm gonna give you 90 days to put together an IT department that works. I'll commit with you on it. I will help anyway I can, but I'm not gonna sign a long-term deal till I know that you can monitor my results. Lack of bureaucracy. The bureaucracy comes in a lot of different forms. It can just be a lot of people covering their own butts, right? So they have all these rules that govern everything. So they never have to make a decision. All I have to do is consult the rule book, right? So decisions are made in a very brainless way. That's one kind of bureaucracy. And it's very dangerous. The problem with it is that if people are operating in that model, you can't just say, okay, from now on everyone be creative. Everyone make the best decision you can make. Screw the rule book. Because he is staffed up and trained a company full of bureaucrats. So it takes remaking the company, rebuilding it from the ground up. The other kind of bureaucracy is kingdom building. And it's really poisoned to a company. It's where each middle level or top level executive has his own little kingdom. And if anybody comes in there, anybody from outside has to be destroyed. Has to be attacked, defamed, destroyed because they threaten this manager in some way. You can see it by the kind of people that report to him. If he hired them, that kind of manager will only hire people who are not as smart as he is. And that's his way of retaining his job. Because he's not a superior or excellent employee himself. And so he hires inferior people and has them under him. And it's kind of a veiled threat to the company. Hey, you think I'm bad? You fire me and old Al is coming up behind me and he's even worse. And it's poisoned in a company. And you'd be amazed at how many companies are structured that way. Another thing along those lines, it's not really bureaucracy, but it has to see an org chart of the company. You want to know who reports to whom. You want to know what the chain of command is. And if they don't have an org chart, that's a red flag. If they have an org chart that has 135 people all reporting to one guy, that's a red flag. That was wise research. The president company would check an inventory or a requisition for toilet paper. There was no decision made in that company that didn't go in front of Martin. So ask for the org chart. It'll tell you a lot. It'll help you identify the key players in the company. War chest, how much money you got? Every company holds back cash reserves. If you have a bad month, you still have to pay the employees. You still have to pay the overhead. It's retained earnings basically that they don't want to pay out of the company and have to pay taxes on. If it's a good company, they're going to have a war chest that's equal to 25 to 50% of their annual revenues. That money can be available for marketing. You can use that money to be a hero. You can take that money and rent mailing lists. You can buy television time, radio time, online media, rent lists online, do easy and sponsorships and so forth. But they need cash if you want to grow them. Now if you're going to go in basically and just hammer the house file, which is perfectly fine, and cream it and get a lot of money out of the house file, that's good. But it's not a long-term relationship. It's something you may want to do once or twice or three times. Walk away with a lot of money and be real happy with it. But if you want to get into a long-term relationship with this client, a partnership, he's got to have enough money to be able to finance the growth of his mailing list. And so that's, and if you want to answer that question, move on. Opportunities. Look at the company. Look at the competition. Where does this company rank in terms of the competition? Is he right at the top? Is it the biggest there is in his industry? Is he somewhere near the bottom? Is he in the middle? How much room for growth is there here? And this is demonstrated room for growth. You look at his competition, how big are they? But you also look at the company. I think at my summit, oh yeah, we were doing hot seat sessions at my summit. And this guy comes up with this direct mail piece that was atrocious. And I think it was Carlton or it could have been Paris. But anyway, he looks at this horrible mail piece and the poor guy standing there. And he goes, I have good news for you. He said, this thing is so bad that if you're making any money at all, you're about to explode the size of your business. So look for those opportunities. It could be in a department he doesn't have that he needs. It could be a staff situation. It could be a new product, a product idea that you have. I am amazed. I was with Guthee Rinker a few years back, Bill Guthee. And they're the big infomercial company. And at the time they had this product, they're proactive now. They have the proactive line. But back then they had Vanuwhites' tooth whitening system. And so I'm at Bill's house. He lives next to Tom Petty in Woodland Hills. And he's married to Victoria Jackson, by the way, who hates me, because I walked into the living room with Bill and she was in curlers in a bathrobe. So I was talking to Bill and I said, hey man, what do you do with these names after they buy the tooth whitening system? What do you mean, do with them? What do you do with the names? I mean, you have all these new customers. He says, well, eventually we're going to offer them more tooth whitening goop or whatever. And I said, so these guys are not going to hear from you again or from Vanuwhite again for a year or so until you offer them more goop. And he goes, yeah, that's pretty much it. And I said, man, that is insane. You're generating, I mean, the juice man juicer, he sold 100, what was it? What was it? I think it was 150,000 units of this $100 juicer. Vanuwhite's tooth whitening system, huge sales. And they were doing absolutely nothing with those names that came in. You see an opportunity like that. You mortgage your first born, you sell your house, you take the assignment because you get a percentage of that and you'll never have to work again. So opportunities, of course, being the big one. But chemistry, desire, list, infrastructure, lack of bureaucracy, war chest. You cover all of those things. You're able to give the guy an A, a B, or even a C plus on all of those categories and you may have a match made in heaven. Now we talked about the questions that you ask at the launch meeting. This is more, I want to talk about relationship. How do you get to the point where you can have the conversation with a guy about why he should give you half of this company or why he should give you 30% of royalties or whatever, of sales or whatever? So these are different questions. This is, let's say, you're hanging out with a guy in his office or something or go to dinner with him or something. How do you come up with your business model? How did you come up with the strategy that we're using now? What else did you try first? Okay, what failed, what worked? Same thing for the offer. Have you tested this? Have you tried other business models or marketing models? Get him talking about his personal experience building the company. If you're doing an acquisition campaign, ask him what other products you plan to sell to these people because I can help you sell more products to them with some of the cues that I'll have in my sales copy for acquisition. I can position you and them in a situation that makes it much easier to convert them. So tell me about that process. Well, telling you about that process is giving you the opportunity to contribute to it. Okay. You continue talking to him, just picking his brain on this. Everyone loves to talk about themselves, so this is not hard to do. How do you sell to these customers that I'm generating for you? Do you do it online? Do you send them direct mail pieces? Do you outbound call to them? Is there a funnel? Is there a regular gauntlet or a honeymoon period? A gauntlet or a honeymoon period would be like a 30-day to pick a number, period of time after a new guy comes on file that he's treated very differently from the rest of the list. Okay. So he's brand new to us, so we're going to pamper him and we're going to get him to love us over the next 30 days, and we're not going to beat him up with offers. Okay. So what does your gauntlet look like? What does the honeymoon period look like with this guy? What's your strategy to monetize that list on an ongoing basis? We have a product of the month basic approach where every month or two months will come along with a brand new product that's really exciting and will do a long launch on the product. That's our business model. What's yours? How do you monetize these people during the year? What do you do to retain them? Somebody calls in and says, cancel my subscription. What do you do? Do you offer them another subscription? Do you offer to extend the subscription to give them more time to make their decision? Do you extend the guarantee period? How do you retain the customer? And what are your key numbers? We'll talk about key numbers before we're done here. But understanding what his benchmarks are in terms of response, average sale, and other things is absolutely critical. But again, this is you being smarter than the fish. Okay. If you're going to catch fish, you've got to be smarter than the fish. So this is you beginning the conversation that's going to lead you into a partnership with this guy. Now it's time to get personal. So far we've been talking about his company. I took my client on a cruise. He and his wife and my wife and I, and I said, we need like three, four days together, or we can just talk, maybe relax, have some fun. So let's take a cruise, the four of us. So we got on this cruise ship. Do not allow the client to select the cruise ship. Big tip. I would have picked a celebrity or Norwegian or best of all, crystal cruise lines. He picked the Warner Brothers cruise. The big red boat. And so we had Wiley Coyote turning down our bed every night. And they retired the boat like a month after. Yeah. This is a cruise ship, but it had eight foot ceilings. Just like, you know, like, it was probably as best as ceilings, you know. And it was, you know, it was, it was fun. But my tip, Wendy, you missed it was don't let the client pick the cruise, right? So, no, but for us, it was a cruise. We could afford it. It made sense. And I wanted to get him away from the phone. I didn't want him being influenced by any of his top executives who were vying for his attention or anything. And I just wanted to pick his brain and see if we could take this to the next level. And that's how I positioned it, you know. I'd asked all these questions about his business model, but now I wanted to know more about him. Want to know what's your personal mission statement? Do you want to, you want to continue working for the rest of your life? You want to build this company up forever? Or do you want to get it to a certain point, sell out and go do something else? I've always thought it was interesting that, you know, if you own a company, chances are nearly 100% of your assets are tied up in that company, right? So why would anybody sell a profitable company? Why would you do it? Well, it's called diversification, you know. It's just a smart move. You get the company to a certain size. He's then able to sell off a chunk of that company, you know, up to 50% without losing control. He's able to sell off a chunk of that company, take that money and go invest it elsewhere. Whether it's government bonds or the stock market or maybe to buy another company. So there's always an opportunity to get a stake in a company from a guy, you know. So maybe he's at that, you ask him this question, he may say, no, no, no, a lot of people, most of them will say, I'd like to get it up to this level and then sell, or at least, you know, take in a partner, you know. And wow, you know, you're thinking that partner's you, you know, and it could be, right? So on that cruise with that client, we would always get up early in the morning because the women wanted to sleep in. So we'd walk around the boat, find a quiet place, sit down, talk. And I said, I want to buy your company from you. And he goes, really? And I said, yeah. And I told him how much I'd pay him. And he goes, wow, well, that's really something to think about. And he sounded really excited about it. And I said, but I want an answer by tomorrow, you know, because if we're not going to do this, I want to know. If we are going to do this, I want to know. So he says, OK. And so then he was really seemed interested. And then the next morning we're talking and I said, well, what would you decide? And I said, OK, I'm going to buy it. I decided that if you want to buy it, I don't want to sell it. You see potential here. I don't see. And therefore I'm going to own the company and you're going to build it and I'm going to get the money. So but I knew right where we stood, you know, and I knew what we were going to do together that dictated what our relationship would be. So a short, medium and long term vision for the company. You may get a blank stare on this one. It's amazing. The Chinese have a 500 year plan. The United States works on three year plans. Most businesses have a, you know, 12 hour plan. And it's what we have to get done today, you know. So but if he doesn't have one, that's a really good sign because what does it do? It gives you the opportunity to work with him to develop a plan. And the cool thing about plans is once you have one, you know exactly what kind of resources you're going to need in order to take him to the next level. And it increases your chances for success in doing those things. Something else I was going to, and it may have come to me. What challenges are we going to have to overcome to achieve your vision? I mean you can see how you're asking these questions you've already identified yourself as his partner, whether he knows it or not. You know, okay, this is what you want. Okay, I'm going to help you do it. Okay, and so you ask these other questions, right? What are your greatest personal frustrations in growing your company? Again, this is personal stuff, but you know, he's going to start talking about problem employees or problems that he has with the corporate structure, you know. And it'll be really good for you to be able to identify the people in the company that are assets, you know, as opposed to liabilities. What's your greatest fear personally? You know, a businessman, a guy that owns a company, this personal fear is going to be related to the company most likely. There might be other things too. Why do you think people buy your product? Why wouldn't they buy this from someone else? You know, what are the most common causes for customer complaints? What are your strongest and weakest managers? You'll know right away. I mean, he knows better than anybody, but personal loyalties may cloud his vision or prevent him from telling you the full truth here, but at least you'll get some good cues. And what have you tried to work in? What have you tried to fail? And I'm talking here about internal functions, how the company works, how the departments work together, how efficient the machine is. Then, you know, $64,000 question, how can I help you get what you want? You know, I'm standing here. I've got skills. I know what I'm doing in these areas. How can I help you get what you want? And that's the beginning of what could be a very long conversation. That's, to me, the funnest part of it, because then he's going to start making connections in his own mind. Well, Clayton knows marketing and he knows copy, and he's got a lot of contacts in this business. And, you know, so he's starting to think about what I can bring to the table as more than just a copywriter at that point. And then the final question is, you know, you wouldn't ask it this way, but what's in it for me, you know? If I, and this is a transactional question like I did with that headline, if I can do these things that you've already told me you desperately want, would it be worth it to you to pay me this for doing that? And more than likely he'll say, yeah, he's just told you this is his greatest desire, his greatest heart's desire, these are the things he wants. Great way to get the conversation starting. Now, you know what he wants, okay? Whether or not you immediately structure a deal together, you can start offering ideas to him that will help him get what he wants. And that's going to bond you even closer to the company and give you more opportunities to work with him, okay? You just do those things, take those simple steps and you're no longer a copywriter. You're a strategic company growth consultant, you know? You're a champion, you're an advocate, you're a partner. And sooner or later partners get compensated like partners, okay? So after that conversation, now you've got the owner on your side, what do you do? Get permission to dig deeper because now you're going to have to go to his IT department and you're going to have to go to different managers and you're going to have to look into his overhead and you're going to have to look into other stuff that's going on the company. You're going to have to interview employees and ask them what's going right, what's going wrong, you know, what could be improved. And they'll talk to you where they might not talk to the owner because they're intimidated. They don't want to take him off and lose their job. So get his permission and have him call managers into a room say, hey, this is Clayton. Clayton's a consultant, you know? He won't say copywriter at this point. Clayton's a consultant and he's going to look into some things, see if he can give us some ideas to accelerate our growth. And so now they all know that it's him asking for this. Otherwise, you'll get pushback and that can get real frustrating. Okay, so you want, one of the first things you want to do at this point is identify benchmarks and this is important for two reasons. Number one or three maybe. Number one, you'll know whether or not their IT department is capable of producing good data. Okay, whether or not they can tell you what the numbers are. Number two, it gives you a foundation from which you'll grow the company. So when you come to the point where you're negotiating your contract, you know where the company is now in all of these areas. Okay, you know what his delivery rate is for his email. You know what his click-through rate is. Conversion rate, orders, average unit of sale, total revenue, and ROI, CPL and so forth. You know what those numbers are. Now if you want to, when you do your structuring of the deal, you can say, or even leading up to it, you can say, well, what if I was able to double your delivery rate in email? You know, what if we were able to, this is a J-Abraham move. What if I was able to just increase each one of these things 10%? The cumulative growth would be a doubling in your company, right? 100% increase if there are 10 things there. But anyway, you'd have 10, whatever it is. But the idea here is, okay, I get 10% more of your emails delivered. I get 10% more people clicking through. Right now your click-through rate is 5%. We're going to go to 5.5%. Eminently doable, right? We're going to raise your conversion rate just 10%. We're going to increase the number of orders we get on house file promotions by 10%. We're going to increase the average sale. Right now it's $49. We're going to make it $54, you know, just by 10%. We're going to increase order frequency among your customers. Instead of ordering from you an average of three times a year, they're going to order from you a 3.3 times per year. Okay? So now this is the beginning of your plan on how to explode this company. Right? But it's also your benchmark. And you can tell your compensation to this if you wanted to. If I do these things, will you give me this? Okay? For offline marketing, it would be the, let's say, direct mail. Or any media, really, television, radio, newspaper ads, whatever. What's the cost? How much are you paying per thousand? You know, how many pieces are you mailing? Or how many eyeballs are you getting right now? What are your typical response rates to your promotions? What's your average sale? Is there a way to get more money out of each sale? Or are you at optimal levels? Have you tested price? What's your total revenue for the company? I mean, that's a big number. Your gross revenue for the year. Don't deduct anything, all the money that's brought in every year. What is the gross amount? Because that's an easy thing to tell your compensation to. And then what is your return on investment on direct mail? Okay? So, or, you know, other offline marketing. More things to ask. Okay? How many orders per year does your average customer place? What is the average order size? How long is a customer with you? You generate a customer in 2005. How many years does that customer continue to be active, continue to make at least one purchase per year from you? Okay? So, that's the customer lifetime, right? What is his lifetime? And then the ultimate number is average customer lifetime value. Now, if the customer stays with you for, typically for seven years, and you can increase that by just one year, okay? On top of all of these other changes, the multiplying effect is just absolutely enormous. So, now, you do that on top of this other stuff, and you have quantum growth. The company takes off like a rocket. Okay, we're going to look at some more math. I waited until 230, so you got that as after-lunch sleepies over with, hopefully. But this is important, and when you're having conversations with a client, knowing this stuff sends a message that you're not, you're a marketer, you're not just a copywriter, but also helps you better understand how to help the client. So, we're going to just define some terms, okay? Campaign cost is total cost to contact the prospects, not including copy or design costs. They never include the creative costs in this number because it's amortized over the period of the campaign. So, if the campaign, you don't know how long a campaign is going to run there, therefore you can't really, it's not fair to put it in. So, you know, what was the total cost? Let's say it's a mailing of a million pieces. What was the total cost of all that, okay? Or it could be the number of prospects contacted, for example, if it's a space ad or something. Cost per impression, okay? It's the total campaign cost we just talked about, divided by the number of impressions. So, now you come up to a cost per thousand number in direct mail, or cost per eyeball, or cost per impression in other media, okay? CPM is cost per thousand. Everyone thinks M means million, it's thousand. Latin, I guess. So, okay, response rate, pretty clear, right? Everyone knows what that is. It's the percentage of people you contacted who actually responded to the offer, okay? A thousand leads are so divided by a hundred thousand impressions total, you have a one percent response rate. Cost per lead we talked about this morning, you know, it cost me five thousand dollars to do the campaign. I got one thousand leads, my CPL is five bucks. And we talked about the ranges for those things that work. Conversion rate, percentage of leads who make a purchase. A lead is someone who hasn't bought anything, right? You just brought in the name because he asked for something free. So, conversion rate is how you do a campaign to those leads. It's how many of those people have now become customers, what percentage of them have become customers as a result of that campaign. You're going to need to know about customer acquisition cost. This is almost never used, so people will look at ROI rather than customer acquisition cost, different way to look at it. They'll also look at sense per name. Phillips was big on sense per name. And that basically is for every name I mailed, how many pennies do I get back? So you want to know that number as a benchmark and you want to monitor it. Average sale, you simply divide total revenues by number of purchases. And it tells you how big the average sale is. And one of the things I love doing in any kind of direct response campaign is, you know, the total response is response rate times average sale. So that gives you two things you can tinker with. You know, you can test things that will get more people to order, and you can also test things that will get each person who's ordering to buy more or to spend more. ROI we talked about yesterday. I don't think we have to hit that again. Average customer lifetime value, that's clear, right? Guys are going to be with me seven years. He's going to spend an average of this much per year. And therefore, his lifetime value is X, right? Yeah, I got that one. Okay, so now it's time to develop your strategy for a double, right? So you have four critical steps. There's only four things you have to do. Okay, in any company, you have to create more customers. You have to persuade each one to order more often. You have to persuade each one to place larger orders, and you have to persuade each one to stay with you longer. So as you're laying out the plan for the company, you will have campaigns designed explicitly to do each one of these things. On the short side of the equation, lowering refunds is also extremely important, and it's something that's fun to look into, because it's quite often an easy thing to do. So then go to your client and say, okay, look, here's what we've got to do. We're going to get 20% more orders, 20% more customers, 20% greater longevity. If we do those things, we will go from $10 million to over $20 million. You have a $10 million company today, we'll go to over 20 if we just do those four things. And if you also do some of the other things we talked about, you can have even more explosive growth. And again, it's quantum growth, because if you increase any one of those numbers, do it more than 20%, now you're looking at really explosive growth, because they build on each other. So first of all, how do you create more customers? You look at new strategies for doing it. Then there's a, you know, just go online and go through the process with people like Rich Sheffrin and other business building websites, and look at how they do Rich's strategic profits, by the way. Look at how they get customers. These guys are cutting ads. This is all they do is study this stuff. So, you know, go to these people that teach business building, the Bizop guys online, and go through their processes, opt in for the easy, and see how they sell to you. You're going to learn an awful lot about doing this. New media, during the, we were actually, this almost never happens, but we were actually able to buy television ads to drive traffic to our American Apocalypse video. The thing was so effective that I could pay the much higher price for offline advertising through television to drive people to see this thing. And we generated a lot of good people that way. Always keep your eye open for ways that you can test something that's proven online. Maybe you should take it and turn it into a direct mail piece. Maybe you should take it, cut it down a little bit, turn it into a full-page newspaper ad. Always be looking for new product ideas. That's the lifeblood of the company. You know, people buy your, you know, product A. Now they're dead weight to you until you have something else that you can sell them. And so, and product development is a whole other subject. It's a whole other weekend, or week maybe, but the idea here is you've got a file of people who have bought product A. What does that mean they also need? What other product that's very similar to it or in the same grain can you offer them? There's something kind of important there. It has to do with positioning. If you have, if you're positioned as a great toothpaste company, your next product should probably be a toothbrush or floss. It probably shouldn't be a hemorrhoid cream. Right? Because in his mind, the prospect sees you as a toothpaste company. He sees you at that end of the body, not the other, you know? Well, and we've all seen these massive misfires, you know, in terms of new products. And the new Coke was probably the most famous one. New offers and new creative. Okay, so new offers is just simply, we need to find out the ultimate definitive offer for this product in this promotion. New creative, that is a constant process. That's, you know, building your team of copywriters. That puts you in my position where you're interviewing the other people in this room and finding people to help you get your stuff done. So if you don't focus on new customers, the company is dead. I think we had this conversation yesterday, right? The list will get old. It'll start, you'll be seeing attrition and your response rates. And instead of growing, the company will go the other way. So building the list, acquiring new customers, acquiring new leads is the single most important thing you can do. It doesn't make the company any money. It's hard to do. It takes constant attention. But it's the most important thing you do. It's a little difficult sometimes because the heroes in a company are the ones that are doing the house file marketing. Because they get to run around and say, hey man, I made six million dollars this month. You know, so they're the heroes. But the real heroes should be the people who are bringing in leads and converting them and making customers. Because that's the future of the company. Next step, focus on increasing order quantity. Okay? So you're looking at increasing contact frequency. I mean, how often am I going to my customers? Absence does not make the heart grow fonder online. You don't ask for money, you don't get money. That was a mantra at Response Sync when I had Total Package. We don't ask for money, we don't get money. So you go to that file aggressively with value-added campaigns that make them want to read your emails, but you go to them once to twice a day. If you have an e-zine, maybe that means three times a day. If you have a morning and afternoon edition of your e-zine, maybe that means four times a day. Okay? So increase contact frequency is a way to increase order quantity, number of orders. New products will do that. New offers will do that. New creative and file segmentation will do that. File segmentation in a nutshell is where you go into the file and say, what did these people bought from me in the past? Okay? Is this a gold investor? Platinum investor? Is this a stock investor? Is this an oil investor? What kind of investor is he? Well, if I have a sub-list of oil investors and I have an oil product, guess how much higher my response rate is going to be to that subset on the file than to the file as a whole. So, you know, if you can have the copyright and creative horsepower to segment the file that way, you can have a huge increase in order frequency because your response on each of those subsets will shoot the moon. Okay? Order size. I think I may cover this in a minute, but what we're thinking about here is basically a marketing calendar, right? And there are basically two kinds of marketing promotions to a house file. I call them vertical and horizontal. Vertical promotions are the ones that everybody gets on a particular day. Horizontal ones are the ones that only certain people get. They're the ones that, you know, you have one for your gold guy, one for your sock guy, one for your heart guy, one for your arthritis guy, one, you know, so forth. So, the vertical ones can be tied to all kinds of things. They can be tied to your prospect's birthday. They can be tied to Easter. They can be tied to a time of year or whatever. So, looking at your year and setting up your marketing calendar, you take those things into account. So, maybe once a week, you pull out all the names who are having a birthday that week and they get a birthday card from your company, right? Very strong bonding. So, you want to think about the file in those two ways. Okay? Premium structure. When I'm deciding whether I want to get one year or two year of a newsletter, the premium structure helps me determine that. You know, what's the best deal for me? So, as a customer. So, I look at my premium structure and I go, you know what? If I added one more premium in the two year, I bet I could get a 10% increase in two year orders. And that would go a long way towards increasing my average unit of sale. Right? So, you'd be looking at that. And so, you know, file segmentation and so forth, right? Customer longevity. A lot of people don't do this, but think about bonding or reward programs. We created discount coupons for our customers at Health Resources. And so, anytime somebody ordered from us, they got a discount coupon so they could save 10% on their next order. They loved it. It was a bonding thing. It was a retention thing. And they wanted to use that 10% off discount coupon, right? So, you know, they went for it. Retention programs. This is a good one if you're selling subscription products, by the way. The retention programs or renewal programs are very hard to do. Hard to make work. And they do work, but they require constant attention. We hit a breakthrough when we found out that when we sold the product, we have one paragraph copy in there that just says, and from now on, to make sure I never miss a single issue and I never have to pay a price increase, when my subscription comes up for renewal, you'll notify me first and then you will, you know, bang my credit card. And then if I don't object, you'll bang my credit card at the same low rate. So now, all of a sudden, 90% of the file is automatically renewing. We don't have to make a big deal about it, right? And it really helped us with our renewal rates. Complaint resolution. What happens when a customer calls in and he's upset about something or he's complaining? There needs to be a very solid, strong way that you can say to that customer, look, you know, I want you to have this $1,000 gift or whatever. Maybe it's a service, maybe it's a, you know, some other product or maybe it's a discount coupon, a big one, to keep him happy, to keep him on board. And then product improvement. Constantly be looking at your products, especially the ones where you have high cancellation rates or high refund rates. Look at those products all the time and challenge the staff, challenge everybody to come up with ways to improve them. There are three types. We're going to go through these kind of fast. We're getting short on time. But there are three types of improvement. The first one is incremental. And you do that by creating a testing culture inside of you. Testing everything consistently. Always looking for ways to get a lift to increase response. You can test all of these things. In any promotion, as we've said, the most important things to test are the headline and lead, the theme, the headline and lead. But beyond that price, media strategy, formats, guaranteed, all of that stuff can increase response. Product improvement. You can take a product that's a pretty good product and you can enhance it. Give them a 13 month subscription instead of 12. Give them a morning and afternoon addition instead of just morning. Give them 10 more pills in every bottle. What can you do to improve each product and make it more competitive? Line extension. You can extend a line. If you've got the Juice Man juicer, you can have Juice Man Jr., which is a travel juicer or something. Just think about it. What can you do to extend the same line? For subscription products, you can intensify the product by turning it into a monthly or even a daily subscription. We've done a lot with bundles. Let me tell you about this really cool offer because you might be able to use it and it works like gangbusters. You go to the file and you say, hey, look guys, we're so proud of these services. This is a subscription service deal. We're so proud of these services that we want you to have them all for 30 days for just $39. You can test drive them yourself. Normally they'd be $10,000 for a year. We want you to be able to test drive them for $39 for 30 days. If you don't like what you see, call us and cancel. Otherwise, beginning next month, we will hit your credit card for whatever the amount is, $400 or whatever it is. Did I cover this yesterday? It works like gangbusters because you want them to call in to cancel because then they get upsold and downsold. It works really well. Think about that kind of thing. That's bundles and memberships. Joint Venture Products. One of the big challenges every company has is products. How do you keep the pipeline full of great new products? When you do, when you have a new product you've developed, how are you going to know if it's going to be a dud or a stud? Russell Martinez said, you don't know. You're taking a gamble. You have friendly competitors who have products that you can sell very well to your list. You may not get to keep all the money, but at least it's a product that you can sell into your own file. There's tons of them. I've told you about the Rosenthal Dilla Weiss where he took 30% of the back end. I told you about Abraham in Icy Hot. I told you about me in Security Rare Coin where I took, you know, it was a $3 million company and we were $160 million after a year. And I had a piece of that. Those kinds of, that's explosive improvement. And you always want to keep your eye out for that kind of an opportunity in a company. At Security Rare Coin, the thing that did it for us was when I realized that that coin was not just a silver coin. That it was history. That it could have been in someone's pocket at the OK Corral. And I started selling it on the basis of if it could talk. And it was a strategic change in that company. And it took us from $360,000 to $3.6 million in one month. And at the end of 12 months, it took us to $160 million. And then just look at every aspect of his business. This is common sense. How can we do this faster? What's slowing you down? How can we do it cheaper? How can we save money on the process? Now we're turning you into a consultant here. Remember, the purpose of this exercise is to make you more than a copywriter. Which means that you have a better shot at becoming a partner and increasing your fees. Report your improvements in the benchmark numbers on a regular basis. Don't ever let your client get the news from his internal people first. You want him to get the news from you. You get the numbers from his IT department. You publish a regular report every quarter. You get this report to the client. You hand it to him personally in a meeting. Sit down and talk to him. Walk him through it. Show him what you've done. Internal people will take credit for what you did in a heartbeat. So he needs to get the news from you. OK, this one is very near and dear to everyone's heart. We've just gone through the process of how you go from zero to hero. How you go from a copywriter working for fees and royalties to a partner in the business who now owns a chunk of it or is getting a nice chunk of the bottom line every year. So changing gears, what happens when you write a flop? We've heard from every writer here, every major writer here. We've heard about how everyone has flops. I bet you the first two years I was doing this, I probably didn't have a single winner. That'd be my guess. I don't remember one anyway. So what do you do? What do you do? The first thing is that you don't skulk off like a loser. You grab it by the teeth and you say, I can't believe this. Well, OK, let's take a look at it. What was the response rate? What was the average sale? You address it as you address any important issue to an intelligent person. These marketing people are smart. So you take responsibility for it. You say, it wasn't the copy. When something flops, it's almost never the copy. It might be the overall theme. It might be the market change or whatever. I think I told you that when O.J. Simpson had his little white Bronco chase, low-speed chase, from that point until he was exonerated, we couldn't get a decent response. And when something fast happens like Katrina, we couldn't get a decent response. So it could be anything. It could be the news, the market. These are the things now. You and your client are going to sit down, and the last thing you want is for him to come to the conclusion that you were the problem. So let's look at what was happening. What was the news? Did it take away from readership of our promotion? What was the market doing at the time? What was happening in my case in the investment marketplace? But in the health market, let's say you're a health writer. What was happening there? Did the government come out with some big study saying the vitamins don't do any good? Just while we were doing our promotion? Was there something going on in the market? How about just the timing of it? What part of the year are we in? The best months for promotion typically are January and September, because those two months, and they're anomalies, I mean they're really, really up there. And the reason is that people want to change gears and change their lives in those two times of the year. In January you got a new year. In September, summer's over, kids are going back to school. Another big change. So it's basically new beginnings. So how did seasonality play in this? Seasonality changes market to market. If you're in a particular market, July might be as hot as month if you're selling boats. So find out the seasonality of the guy's market niche. And could that be a part of it? The theme, 9 times out of 10, that's going to be it. 9 times out of 10 is just going to be guys, the theme. And 9 times out of 10, your client will have given you the theme. And so it's his fault, which is always nice. The offer could be a lousy offer. And if he hasn't tried it before, this particular offer, by offer, I mean the free gifts you get for buying, the price, the discounts, the upsell price, the guarantee, the terms of the guarantee, and all that stuff. So look at all of that. Is this untested stuff? Could it have been the offer? And then just the execution of the campaign. You remember when Marcel was talking about it, she did this great campaign and got zero orders. And the guy didn't want to call her and tell her this because they were looking at technical issues. Were the emails, you know it's bad when they're saying, is this on? When they're going, okay, did our emails even get delivered? Evidently your copy isn't doing any better than nothing. So maybe the emails didn't even get delivered. Maybe there was technical glitch. Maybe the links didn't work. Maybe the order page glitched. And if you find out it's something like that, it's always a godsend. Because if I'm online and something like that happens, my next email to that file is going to be, we're so sorry. Yesterday we sent you the email below and there was all of this problem and you probably didn't get it, so I'm sending it again. That line, as a subject line, online, there are two things that really work well. Number one is we're so sorry. We've moved again. We messed this up. Please forgive us. That kind of a subject line. And the other one is any, the word weird, the word weird, one weird trick, one weird secret, one weird anything, and people just, they're all over it. You know, and you'll see the ads online that use that a lot. All right, so here's some tools. Now what I've done in this session basically is given you a lot of ideas for being more than a copywriter. If you want to pursue this route, and I strongly suggest that you do, in addition to your other studies, remember product launch formula is very important to learn. Secondly, over the last two days you've gotten a ton of information. You've been drinking out of a fire hose. You've gotten snippets of ideas, tools, out the wazoo. It's going to take you some time to internalize some of the stuff that you have learned here. Take your notes home, okay? Put them into, you know, take them off out of your binder and put them in a Word document, right? The next step is to create a plan. You need to know where to go from here. How are you going to use all of this information? Okay? And it's different for each one of you. I would love to give each one of you a customized plan that says, here's what you have to do to get customers. Here's what you have to do to build a successful copywriting business. But based on who you are and who your client is and what your personal goals are, that just wouldn't be possible. There are too many variables for me to do that. So what I would recommend is that you go home, get your notes together, and then create a schedule for yourself, a calendar. These are the things that I have to do to grow my copywriting business. These are the things that I'm going to do over the next 60 days. And 60 days from today, I'm going to have a copywriting business as kicking butt. My phone's going to be ringing. I will already be doing deals with people. Have that image in your mind. What steps do you personally have to do it? Take this idea, run with it. Have a begin date, have an end date, and do what's on this calendar. You don't do it, I can't help you. But if you do this stuff, you're going to grow your company. It's just going to happen.
